title: Ironsworn: Starforged - Review - when a well-made thing isn't for me (solo RPG)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PxyjUIyE5YI

The trilogy closes with a candid review: Daniel (@dungeondive) sat down for session three of Starforged, stared at the table, made coffee, came back, stared again, and finally admitted the campaign was over. The world he'd built was great. The character was great. He just couldn't keep moving through the rules. He's careful to separate "this isn't for me" from "this isn't good" — and spends a chunk of the review on what he genuinely admires.

The praise: Shawn Tomkin's free PDF kit (asset cards, sheets, the entire move list) is a model for accessibility, especially next to indie games where finding a character sheet means emailing the designer. Joshua Meehan's art is striking — Daniel screencaps the waterfall-and-worm illustration as desktop wallpaper. The Truths worksheets are his favourite session-zero tool full stop; he wants Mörk Borg to have its own version. The book itself is well-organised and eases you in.

The breaking point: the moves. Forty-plus of them, eight in combat alone, with the wrong choice costing mechanical progress on top of narrative momentum. He sets Starforged beside Fallen (one-page solo), We Deal in Lead, and his preferred Solitary Defilement for Mörk Borg — itself Ironsworn-inspired, but distilled to a handful of moves — and concludes the leaner systems suit how he wants to play. His copies are going.

----

Daniel's split-the-difference verdict — admires Starforged, can't play it — comes down to the move count overwhelming the fiction. Where do you sit on the spectrum between Starforged's structured depth and a one-page oracle like Fallen's?
